coredata.dc:title: Rational preparation of anti-water phosphorescent carbon-dots and flake C3N4 composites through microwave-heating method for multiple data encryption
coredata.dc:creator: Bao, Xin
Liu, Enshan
Yuan, Xi
Wang, Hui
Li, Haibo
Qu, Songnan
coredata.dc:description: The development of anti-water phosphorescent carbon dots (CDs) based materials is of great practical interest. Herein, we report a rational design of CDs based composite with anti-water phosphorescence by controlling a one-step microwave assisted heating of green fluorescent CDs and urea. Upon increasing the microwave-heating temperature, urea was gradually converted to C3N4, in which the CDs were uniformly embedded in the C3N4 matrix. The fluorescence/phosphorescence intensity of these CDs based composites are determined by the morphologies of the C3N4, which can be adjusted by controlling the microwave heating time. More importantly, the combining CDs in C3N4 not only promote the phosphorescence emission but also prevent the phosphorescence quenching by water, leading to stable phosphorescence emission in aqueous environment. Based on the anti-water phosphorescence properties of these CDs based composites, a concept of multiple data encryption was demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Anti-water phosphorescence emission
Matrix structure
Microwave-heating
Multiple data encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs based phosphorescent composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM and HRTEM images of CDs. b) Excitation-emission maps of CD aqueous solution. c) Schematic of the fabrication process and thermal images of CDs and urea aqueous solution under 750 W microwave heating at different time.

a) Brightfield, fluorescence (Ex: 365 nm) and phosphorescence (after turning off 365 nm UV light at 0.5 s) images of CDs composite powders after different microwave heating time. b) Absorption spectra of CDs aqueous solution, and CDs, CD@Urea powders, sample-135, sample-150, sample-165, sample-180, sample-195, sample-225 powders. c) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs composite powders with different microwave heating time (excited at 360 nm). d) Peak fluorescence intensities of these CDs composite powders at 360 nm excitation. e) Fluorescence decay curves of these CDs composite powders monitored at 510 nm after 360 nm light irradiation. f) Phosphorescence emission spectra of these CDs composite powders (excited at 360 nm). g) Peak phosphorescence intensities of these CDs composite powders at 360 nm excitation. h) Phosphorescence decay curves of these CDs composite powders monitored at 510 nm after 360 nm light irradiation.
a) SEM images of the CD@Urea, sample-165 and sample-225. b) XRD patterns of CDs composite powders with different microwave heating time.
High-resolution C1s XPS spectra of a) CD@Urea, b) sample-165 and c) sample-225. N1s XPS spectra of d) CD@Urea, e) sample-165 and f) sample-225.

Proposed mechanism of the photophysical processes taking place in composites heated in different times. “Abs”, “Fluo.“, “Phos.“, and “Vib.” represent “absorption”, “fluorescence”, “phosphorescence”, and “vibrational relaxation” processes, respectively.

a) Brightfield, fluorescence and phosphorescence photographs of sample-165 and sample-225 powders in water. b) Phosphorescence spectra of sample-165 before and after water spray. Insert are the phosphorescence images of pattern written with sample-165@PVA on a glass before and after water spray. c) Schematic presentation of a multiple encrypted patterns using sample-195, commercial fluorescent dye (Flu. Dye) and CD@PVA phosphorescent composites via UV on/off before and after water spray.

head.ce:title: Rational preparation of anti-water phosphorescent carbon-dots and flake C3N4 composites through microwave-heating method for multiple data encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, Jilin Normal University, Changchun, 130103, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, Jilin Normal University, Changchun 130103, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Joint Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education, Institute of Applied Physics and Materials Engineering, University of Macau, Taipa, Macau, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Joint Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education
Institute of Applied Physics and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Joint Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education, Institute of Applied Physics and Materials Engineering, University of Macau, Taipa, Macau, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of anti-water phosphorescent carbon dots (CDs) based materials is of great practical interest. Herein, we report a rational design of CDs based composite with anti-water phosphorescence by controlling a one-step microwave assisted heating of green fluorescent CDs and urea. Upon increasing the microwave-heating temperature, urea was gradually converted to C3N4, in which the CDs were uniformly embedded in the C3N4 matrix. The fluorescence/phosphorescence intensity of these CDs based composites are determined by the morphologies of the C3N4, which can be adjusted by controlling the microwave heating time. More importantly, the combining CDs in C3N4 not only promote the phosphorescence emission but also prevent the phosphorescence quenching by water, leading to stable phosphorescence emission in aqueous environment. Based on the anti-water phosphorescence properties of these CDs based composites, a concept of multiple data encryption was demonstrated.

Anti-water phosphorescent carbon-dots (CDs) and flake C3N4 composites were rationally prepared from CDs and urea by controlling the microwave heating time. Based on the tunable phosphorescent properties of these CDs and C3N4 composites, multiple data encryption was demonstrated.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Phosphorescent materials have aroused considerable interest in the field of bio-imaging, sensing, data encryption and so on due to their long service life of luminescence, ability to be easily captured by naked eyes, and high signal-to-noise ratio [1–7]. However, the phosphorescence from nanomaterials is usually quenched in aqueous solutions due to the presence of dissolved oxygen which is a strong phosphorescence quencher in water. The commercial phosphorescent materials containing rare earth elements, such as Yb and Ir [8–10], are expensive and environmental unfriendly, which undergo serious phosphorescence quenching in aqueous solution. In some metal-free organic phosphorescent material systems, phosphorescence emission can be realized in aqueous environment [11–14], but they also suffer poor luminescence stability and complicated preparation processes. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop novel anti-water phosphorescent materials featuring low cost, easy synthesis, environmental protection, and high stability.

As an emerging luminescent carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn considerable attention in a variety of applications due to their advantages of low cost, low toxicity, tunable fluorescence emission, and facile preparation [15–23]. In recent years, many studies have proven that CDs or their composites are available for obvious phosphorescence emissions, arousing increasing attention in bio-imaging, data encryption and so on [24–28]. Usually, the phosphorescence from CDs can be realized by embedding CDs in some matrices (such as PVA [29], urea [30], silica [31], and cyranuric acid [32]) through the reduction of the proportion of non-radiative transitions. However, the phosphorescence from most CDs-based materials is unstable in the humid environment, which can be easily quenched by water. In several recent works, CDs and matrix (such as SiO2 and cyanuric acid) have been combined to realize phosphorescence emission in aqueous solution [33–36], but the poor luminescence stability in complex environments (such as in hot water) hinders their further development. Therefore, it is desired to develop new CDs-based materials with anti-water phosphorescence, which has significant value in data encryption.

Carbon nitride (C3N4) is a common catalyst material, which can also be used as the matrix of luminescent composites [37,38]. In our previous work, we prepared a new kind of CD@g-C3N4 phosphor with high PLQY based on microwave-heated green emissive CDs and urea. In this work, the reaction conditions were optimized and improved anti-water phosphorescent materials based on CDs were developed. The fluorescent and phosphorescent properties of these composites are determined by the structure of the C3N4 matrix, which could be tuned by controlling the carbonization degree (microwave-heating time). More importantly, the covalent bond between CDs and C3N4 could tightly confine CDs, and substantially protect the radiative transition from the triplet state, therefore, these CDs-based materials exhibit stable phosphorescence emission in aqueous solution. Based on the excellent performance of these CDs and C3N4 composites, a new concept of multilevel fluorescence/phosphorescence data encryption is developed.

Urea (99%) was purchased from Aladdin, citric acid (99.5%) and polyvinyl alcohol were purchased from Macklin. All chemicals were directly used without further purification. Deionized water used in the experiments was purified by a purification system.

3 g citric acid and 3 g urea were co-dissolved in 10 mL deionized water in a 100 mL beaker, then the solution in beaker were heated in a 750 W microwave oven for 300 s. Dark-brown solid in beaker was acquired and re-dispersed in deionized water, after the purified by centrifugation and freeze-dried the CDs powders were acquired.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs based phosphorescent composites
ce:section.ce:para: 0.05 g CD powders and 5 g urea were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water in a 100 mL beaker, then the mixed solution was heated in a 750 W microwave oven. Phosphorescent powders were acquired after heating for 135 s, 150 s, 165 s, 180 s, 195 s and 225 s, which we named Sample-135, Sample-150, Sample-165, Sample-180, Sample-195, and Sample-225 respectively. And powder of CD@Urea was acquired through drying CDs and urea mixed solution at 60 °C.

Phosphorescent inks were obtained by mixed 0.1 g composites (Sample-165, Sample-180 or Sample-195) powder in 1.5 mL PVA aqueous solution and then ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. We acquired mixed solution which can be directly used as security ink for waterproof phosphorescence data encryption.

CD@PVA composites were synthesized as our previous work [29]. 0.5 mg of CD powder was dissolved in 2.5 mL aqueous solution containing 10 wt% PVA aqueous solution, and the mixture were annealed at 200 °C to acquire the phosphorescent composites.

Time-resolved fluorescence and phosphorescence decay curves were collected at 510 nm, under 360 nm excitation. Average luminescence life-times were calculated using the following equation (1):
τ avg
=
∑
a i
τ i 2
∑
a i
τ i

Time-resolved luminescence decay curves were fitted with tri-exponential functions. For the phosphorescence spectra, time between flashes is 2000 ms, flash count is 25, initial delay is 10 ms, and sample window is 500 ms. For the phosphorescence lifetimes curves, time between flashes is 4100 ms, flash count is 10, initial delay is 2 ms, sample window is 3500 ms, delay increment is 20 ms.

TEM images were obtained on a FEI Tecnai-G2-F20 transmission electron microscope. SEM images were obtained on a JSM 5600LV scanning electron microscope. Lifetimes and fluorescence/phosphorescence spectra were carried out by using a spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3) with time-correlated single-photon counting lifetime spectroscopy system and phosphorescent system. UV–visible absorption spectra were collected from a Shimadzu UV-3101PC spectrophotometer. TGA was performed on an American TA Q500 analyzer under a nitrogen atmosphere, with a flow rate of 100 mL min−1. FT-IR spectra were obtained with a Perkin–Elmer spectrometer. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) data were collected on a Siemens D5005 diffractometer. XPS analysis was performed on a Thermo Fisher Scientific Escalab spectrometer using Mg as the excitation source.

CDs were prepared by using a simple microwave-heating method, as reported in our previous work [39]. Fig. 1 a shows that the morphology of CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The diameters of the CDs range from 1 to 3 nm, and HRTEM reveals the lattice fringes to be 0.21 nm, which is consistent with (100) lattice planes of graphitic carbon [40]. The fluorescence excitation-emission map of CD aqueous solution was shown in Fig. 1b, which has a green emitting center. CD composite powders were also obtained from the synthesis between urea and CDs precursors in water via a one-step microwave treatment with different heating time. As shown in Fig. 1c, solid composites were acquired when heating the mixture solution of CDs and urea for 135, 150, 165, 180, 195, and 225 s. The powder after grinding is named as Sample-135, Sample-150, Sample-165, Sample-180, Sample-195, and Sample-225, respectively. CDs@Urea was acquired through drying CD and urea mixed solution at 60 °C. The curve of reaction temperature of these heating processes was shown in Fig. S1, specifically, at the initial stage of the microwave heating (0–130 s), the temperature of mixed solution was no more than 100 °C due to water boiling in beaker; after heating for 130–135 s, water boiled, and the temperature of mixtures in beaker rose over 100 °C; finally, the temperature exceeded 200 °C at around 165–180 s.

Some samples exhibit excellent phosphorescent properties, that is, cyan phosphorescence can be easily observed with the naked eye from Sample-150, 165, 180, 195 and 225 when the UV light is turned off. The fluorescent and phosphorescent brightness first become stronger and then weaker with the increase of carbonization degree (Fig. 2 a). Absorption spectra of CD aqueous solution, CD powder, CD@Urea, and these CD composite powders were further investigated, as shown in Fig. 2b. The absorption bands of all CD composite powders are broader than that of CD aqueous solution, but narrower than that of CD powder, and the curves of Sample-135 are similar to that of CD@Urea. Fluorescence spectra of these CD composite powders were shown in Fig. 2c (under 360 nm excitation), these powders synthesized with different microwave-heating times exhibit a similar fluorescence emission with the center at 510 nm. It can be seen from the fluorescence intensity of these samples that the properties are related to microwave heating time (carbonization degree): only weak phosphorescence signal could be observed from CD@urea and the composites with short heating time (such as Sample-135); with the increase of heating time, signal intensity becomes stronger, and Sample-165 has the strongest fluorescence intensity among these powders; when the heating time is over 165 s, signal intensity decreases with the continuous increase of the heating time (Fig. 2d). The composite of Sample-165 has the highest fluorescence quantum yield of 27.9% (Fig. S2). The time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of these powders (Fig. 2e) further reveal that the fluorescence emission is associated with the carbonization degree of the CDs and urea. It is worth noting that phosphorescent emission of these CD composites has shown a similar phenomenon to the fluorescence, which changed as a result of the variation of the carbonization degree. As shown in Fig. 2f, under 360 nm excitation, Sample-150, 165, 180, 195 and 225 exhibiting phosphorescence emission band peaked at 510 nm, but no obvious phosphorescence emission signal could be observed in the samples of CD@Urea and Sample-135. Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra reveal that most of these composite powders inherit the emission from the CDs. The phosphorescence intensity increased as the heating time increases, and the composite prepared at 165 s (Sample-165) displayed the strongest intensity. However, with the further increase of heating time, the phosphorescence intensity decreased significantly (Fig. 2g). The phosphorescence emission colors of samples show no obvious change with the deepening of carbonization degree. Fig. 2h shows the time-resolved phosphorescence decay curves of these samples heated with different times (excited at 360 nm and collected at 510 nm), the longest average phosphorescence lifetime is 532 ms (Sample-165).

To clarify the form of mixture in these CD-based composites, phase structures were characterized by using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and powder X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the composites exhibit different morphology and structure with different heating time. As can be seen from SEM spectra, Sample-165 are composed of nanosheets, while Sample-225 consists of bulk nanomaterials. According to XRD patterns (Fig. 3b) of CD@Urea, Sample-135, 150, 165, 180 and 195 powders have a similar reflection corresponding to urea, but the reflection intensity decreases with the increase of the reaction time; the (002) diffraction peak of C3N4 at 27.8° appears in Sample-165, and the intensity increases afterwards; the curves of Sample-225 are similar to that of C3N4 prepared from urea in a tube furnace, as reported before [37] (Fig. S3). Results of TEM and XRD patterns demonstrate different structure of matrices in these composites: in the initial heating reaction, the matrices were mainly compounded with the recrystallization urea (such as CD@Urea); then, the flake C3N4 in small size was formed with the increase of the heating time (such as Sample-165); finally, with the carbonization degree going down deeply, the flake C3N4 transformed into the bulk structure C3N4 in large size.

Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were further studied to confirm the structure and surface state of these CD-based composites. FTIR spectra of Sample-135, 165, 195 and 225 were shown in Fig. S4, and the peaks centering at 1642 cm−1 correspond to the bending vibrations of N–H. Besides, the intensity decreases with the increase of the carbonization degree [26]. XPS spectra of CD@Urea, Sample-165 and Sample-225 were shown in Fig. S5, and the deconvoluted XPS spectra of C1s and N1s of CD@Urea, Sample-165 and Sample-225 were also performed and compared (Fig. 4 ). For high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 4 a-c), CD@Urea, Sample-165 and Sample-225 were all deconvoluted into three peaks at 287.9, 285.4 and 283.9 eV corresponding to CO/CN, C–OH and C–N/C–C, respectively. It can be clearly seen that the ratio of C–N/C–C and C–OH bonds decrease, while the ratio of CO/CN bond increases with the increase of the microwave heating time. The N1s XPS spectra of CD@Urea only have one peak at 398.7 eV for pyridinie N, but Sample-165 and Sample-225 can be fitted to two peaks at 398.7 and 399.8 eV for pyridinie N and NC–N, respectively. It could be seen that with the increase of the degree of reaction, more pyridinic N was converted into NC–N. High-resolution O1s XPS spectra of CD@Urea, sample-165 and sample-225 were shown in Fig. S6. The results of FTIR and XPS data further verify that urea is transformed to C3N4 and plays the role of matrix in the composites under microwave heating.

Based on the above results, the possible mechanisms for phosphorescence changes in these composites were proposed, as shown in Fig. 5 . Phosphorescence originates from the radiative transitions of triplet state in CDs, and the function groups (such as CO and O–H) on the surface of CDs play a key role to encourage the phosphorescence emission [28]. However, in most cases, the triplet state relaxes the excitation energy through non-radiative transitions (mainly the vibration dissipation) [41]. In the initial reaction, CD@Urea powder is the mixture of CDs and recrystallization urea, and fluorescence of CDs is weakened due to the aggregation-induced quenching. There are no intersystem crossing processes between singlet state and triplet state, therefore, no phosphorescence emission could be observed in CD@Urea. However, under microwave-heating, urea is gradually converted into C3N4 through deamination and dehydration reaction, which is followed by the covalent coupling reactions between CDs and C3N4. For Sample-165 composite, CDs are embedded in the flake C3N4 in small size, which prevents the aggregation-induced quenching of CDs, and the crystalline environment can effectively enhance the ISC progress, and reduce the vibration dissipation. In addition, the covalent bonds also played a key role in the stabilization of the triplet states of CDs, and the suppression of the non-radiative transition [36], thereby greatly promoting the phosphorescence emission, therefore, strong fluorescence/phosphorescence emission was acquired in Sample-165 powders. Finally, with further microwave-heating, flake C3N4 is transformed into bulk C3N4 in large size, and CDs are embedded in situ in the matrix (such as Sample-225). With prolonged microwave-heating (over 200 °C), the function groups on the surface of CDs (CO and O–H) undergo dehydration and carbonization (Fig. S6), and carbon cores are gradually fused in the matrix, which, in general, are more prone to the translation into part of the bulk bulk C3N4. Thus, energy transfers and losses among CDs in the bulk C3N4 were enhanced, which significantly quench the luminescence and phosphorescence from CDs in the composites.

Covalent bonds between CDs and flake C3N4 provide a great protection for the radiative transition from the triplet state, therefore, the composites of Sample-165, 180, 195 and 225 remain stable phosphorescence emission in aqueous solution. Fig. 6 a shows the immersion of Sample-165 and Sample-225 powders in water, and an obvious phosphorescence signal from the powders could be observed by eye. It is worth noting that the supernatant in the tube of Sample-165 has a green fluorescence emission, which could be illustrated by the fact that some CDs have not been Covalently embedded in or have escaped from flake C3N4. However, the fluorescence of aqueous solution in Sample-225 tube is weaker, indicating that most of the CDs were fused in bulk C3N4.

Based on the properties above, a new kind of anti-water phosphorescent ink was developed based on these composites with stable phosphorescence emission in humid environment. As shown in Fig. 6b for the inset, the pattern of “CD” was written on a glass sheet using the phosphorescent ink prepared from Sample-165, and the film was annealed at 60 °C for drying. Upon turning off the UV light, an obvious phosphorescence signal of “CD” could be identified by eye. After water spray, phosphorescence emission from the pattern shows no obvious change. And a similar phosphorescence emission spectrum could be collected before and after the water spray. A concept of fluorescence/phosphorescence multiple data encryption was proposed based on the anti-water phosphorescent ink prepared by Sample-195 powder and PVA aqueous solution, and the CD@PVA phosphorescent composite reported previously by us [29] and commercial fluorescent dye were also employed. The encryption principle is illustrated in Fig. 6c, and the data of “UREA”, “ots” and “CD” is written on a glass slide using fluorescence dye, CD@PVA, and anti-water phosphorescent ink, respectively. Only green fluorescence patterns of indistinguishable information are observed under UV light irradiation. After the extinguishment of UV light, the emissive signal of “UREA” disappears, and the characters written with anti-water ink (CD) and CD@PVA (ots) display phosphorescence emission, which is the first level of the encrypted information (CDots). Then, the glass slide is sprayed with water, only “CD” exhibits phosphorescence emission, and the data of “ots” is quenched after the turning off of the UV light, which is the second level of the encrypted information (CD). Therefore, the multi-data encryption is easily realized based on this anti-water phosphorescent composite, demonstrating a great potential in advanced security application.

In summary, an optimized CD-based composite with anti-water phosphorescence was prepared by using a simple microwave-assisted heating method. With different microwave heating time, urea in raw materials was transformed into C3N4 with different structures and sizes, which acts as matrixes in these composites. CDs embedded in matrix decrease the non-radiative decay, and the covalent bonds between CDs and flake C3N4 promote the radiative transition of triplet state in CDs, leading to the enhanced phosphorescence emission. The fluorescence/phosphorescence intensity was first strengthened and then weakened with the change of composite structure, and the CDs embedded in flake C3N4 in small size (Sample-165) had the strongest fluorescence/phosphorescence emission. More importantly, the covalent bonds also protect the triplet state in CDs, and the phosphorescence could not be quenched by water. A new concept of multi-data encryption was proposed based on these anti-water CD-based composites, which exhibits great potential in advanced security application.

Xin Bao designed and conducted the experiments. Enshan Liu and Haibo Li participated in data analysis. Hui Wang participated in sample characterizations. Xin Bao wrote the draft of the manuscript, and Xi Yuan and Songnan Qu provided the revisions. All the authors contributed to the discussion.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by the Science and Technology Project of Jilin Provincial Education Department (JJKH20220424KJ), the Development of Science and Technology of Jilin Province (20210101161JC), the Science and Technology Development Fund of Macau SAR (0040/2019/A1, 0073/2019/AMJ).
